query: use of contralateral knee in assessing joint line position for knee revision surgery

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Replacement Techniques -- Technique or Treatment -- 1) Measured Resection
retrieved_docs.content: Anatomic landmarks are used to reference a neutral femoral implant rotation. Transepicondylar axis (TEA): Connects lateral condyle prominence to sulcus of the medial epicondyle. Implants placed parallel to this will provide a rectangular flexion gap and result in improved patellofemoral tracking, femorotibial kinematics, and coronal stability. Whiteside line (AP axis of the femur): Starts at the center of the trochlear sulcus and ends posteriorly at the midpoint of the intercondylar notch and typically is perpendicular to TEA. Posterior condylar axis (PCA): Goes based upon a line connecting posterior condyles; in most cases, externally rotating the posterior condylar axis by 3 degrees will create a line parallel to the TEA. This, however, can be very deceiving in patients with a valgus knee and hypoplastic lateral femoral condyle and can lead to malrotation of the implant.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Arthroplasty Techniques -- Technique or Treatment -- Procedural Steps
retrieved_docs.content: Next, the flexion gap is attained by using an anteroposterior sizing guide positioned with respect to the bony landmarks on the femur (usually the Whiteside line or the native transepicondylar axis). Depending on the surgeon's preference for anterior or posterior referencing, the flexion gap is established and fine-tuned using system-specific incremental sizing adjustments available with the cutting guides. In addition, it is essential to visualize the flexion gap before making the bony cuts and to ensure proper soft tissue balancing.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Replacement Techniques -- Preparation -- Preoperative Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Plain radiography typically provides sufficient detail, and further studies are typically not necessary. Weight-bearing AP, lateral and sunrise views should be routinely performed. AP hip to ankle x-rays allow for evaluation of extra-articular deformities and allow for assessment of the mechanical axis. Radiographic markers can be placed during imaging to allow for the use of templating software to assess for estimated implant sizing and positioning. A lateral patellar shift of more than 3mm was an independent risk factor for patellar maltracking. Images should be interpreted to assess for Insall-Salvati ratio, a preexisting slope of the tibial plateau, and coronal alignment. Custom implants are made based on CT or MRI sequences, and these studies should be performed when a custom implant is desired. Custom implants can be particularly helpful in patients with significant deformity where intramedullary or extra-medullary guides will not work, as well as in patients with preexisting hardware.

retrieved_docs.title: Patellar Instability -- Introduction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging: Radiographic examination will divulge several factors. [1] Radiographs will rule out loose bodies Most common is the medial patellar facet Lateral femoral condyle AP radiographs Best for evaluating overall lower extremity alignment Lateral radiographs Patellar height (Patella Alta versus Baja) Blumensaats line should extend to the inferior pole of the patella at 30 degrees of knee flexion Multiple ratios can be calculated and give an idea about the level of the patella. Ideally, the following ratio should be calculated with the knee in 30 degrees of flexion. Either on a lateral radiograph, Sagittal CT, or MRI images. Insall-Salvati ratio  (0.8 - 1.2) It is the ratio of the patellar tendon length to the length of the patella  (Figure 2) If the ratio is >1.2, this indicates Patella Alta Blackburn-Peel ratio  (0.5-1) It is the ratio of the perpendicular distance between the tibial plateau and patellar articular surface to the length of the patella articular surface. (Figure 3) A ratio >1 indicates Patella Alta Caton-Deschamps - (0.6-1.3) It is the ratio of the distance between the most inferior point of the patella articular surface to the anterior angle of the tibial plateau and the length of the patellar articular surface. ( Figure 4). A ratio > 1.3 indicates Patella Alta The Caton-Deschamps and Blackburn-Peel measurements have higher reliability and can show change after a tibial tubercle osteotomy is performed Patellar tilt Trochlear dysplasia Crossing sign - seen on lateral radiograph, the trochlear groove lies in the same plane as the anterior border of the lateral femoral condyle Represents a flat trochlear groove Double contour sign - the anterior border of the lateral femoral condyle lies anterior to the anterior border of the medial femoral condyle Represents a convex trochlear groove/hypoplastic medial femoral condyle Supratrochlear spur Sunrise/merchant views Best assessment for patellar tilt Lateral patellofemoral angle A line parallel to the lateral patellar facet and a line drawn across the posterior femoral condyles The normal angle is >11 degrees opening laterally Congruence angle is an index of subluxation Measured from a line through the apex of the patella to a line bisecting the trochlea If the congruence angle is lateral to the congruence line, it is considered positive If the congruence angle is medial to the congruence line, it is considered negative The normal angle is < (-)6 meaning the more positive the angle, the more subluxed the patella is laterally CT scan Evaluates femoral anteversion Evaluation of tibial rotation TT-TG distance (tibial tubercle to trochlear groove) Must be measured on axial images - it is calculated by taking a line on axial CT perpendicular to the posterior femoral condyles through the trochlear notch and a line through the middle of the tibial tubercle TT-TG distance is normally around 9 mm [11] TT-TG distance > 20mm is abnormal and has > 90% association with patellar instability [12] MRI Evaluation of loose bodies Osteochondral lesions The medial patellar facet is the most common Lateral femoral condyle bone bruising Most of the traumatic lesions occur during re-location impact Best for assessing MPFL [13] Location of injury The most common injury occurs at the femoral origin (Schottles point) Patellar attachment Midsubstance Combination

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Replacement Techniques -- Technique or Treatment -- 2) Gap Balancing
retrieved_docs.content: Traditionally associated with PS implants, but can be used for CR as well. Before any bony cuts or soft tissue release, the removal of all osteophytes is necessary; this is particularly important in gap balancing because removing osteophytes after balancing will change the ligamentous tension and will affect flexion/extension gaps. Typically a proximal tibial cut is performed then lamina spreaders are placed on medial and lateral sides to assess tension on both sides in extension. Structures are released until a neutral alignment, and symmetric extension gap are achieved. The same steps are then performed with the knee at 90 degrees of flexion. A rectangular flexion gap equal to the extension gap is desired, the posterior femoral condyle cuts are then performed. Joint line elevation is a potential pitfall of this technique and can lead to abnormal contact forces on the patellofemoral joint as well as mid-flexion instability.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Arthroplasty Techniques -- Technique or Treatment -- Procedural Steps
retrieved_docs.content: Next, the proximal tibia is cut utilizing either an intramedullary or extramedullary guide to cut the bone perpendicular to the tibial axis (or within 2° to 3° of varus for surgeons aiming for an "anatomic" TKA procedure). Preferably, an intramedullary guide and a perpendicular tibial cut are utilized. Rotation is set by referencing the medial one-third of the tibial tubercle (proximally) and a point slightly medial to the center of the ankle joint (distally). This alignment is also cross-referenced with the second ray of the foot and the tibial crest.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Arthroplasty Techniques -- Technique or Treatment -- Hot Topics in TKA Techniques
retrieved_docs.content: Measured resection technique: Measured resection technique involves creating bone cuts independently of soft tissue tension. In this technique, bony landmarks are used to set the femoral component rotation, such as the transepicondylar axis, [26] the anteroposterior axis, [27] [28] and the posterior condylar axis. However, determining the transepicondylar axis intraoperatively has shown inconsistency in multiple studies. [26] [29] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Knee Arthroplasty(Archived) -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanical axis of the femur, defined by a line drawn from the center of the femoral head to the center of the knee, is 3 degrees valgus to the vertical axis. The anatomic axis of the femur, defined by a line bisecting the femoral shaft, is 6 degrees valgus to the mechanical axis of the femur and 9 degrees valgus to the vertical axis. [5] The proximal tibia is oriented to 3 degrees of varus. The varus position of the proximal tibia, along with the offset of the hip center of rotation, results in the weight-bearing surface of the tibia being parallel to the ground. The sagittal alignment of the proximal tibia is sloped posteriorly approximately 5 to 7 degrees. The asymmetry of the natural bony anatomy maintains the alignment of the joint and ligamentous tension.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Replacement Techniques -- Technique or Treatment -- 1) Measured Resection
retrieved_docs.content: Most surgeons desire a neutral mechanical axis, and the femur is cut in 5 to 7 degrees of valgus with approximately 9 to 10 mm of bony resection distally. The tibia is cut perpendicularly to the tibial axis. Typically an intramedullary guide is utilized on the femur, and an extramedullary guide is utilized on the tibia. The anatomic slope of the tibial plateau is restored if a CR implant is to be used. Should be a neutral tibial plateau cut if a PS implant is being used due to the risk for the CAM jump phenomenon with an increased tibial slope.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Arthroplasty Techniques -- Technique or Treatment -- Procedural Steps
retrieved_docs.content: After performing the tibial cut, the extension gap is assessed. A spacer block is then inserted with the knee in full extension, and the overall balance of the knee is evaluated using an alignment rod to facilitate and confirm the achieved varus-valgus and tibial slope parameters.

retrieved_docs.title: Arthroplasty Knee Unicompartmental -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The ideal position of the femoral component is central or slightly lateral on the femoral condyle to optimize tracking with the tibial component. [1] For lateral UKA, femoral component impingement on the patella has been described. [21] These were noted to be associated with anterior placement and oversizing of the femoral component. The sulcus terminalis of the lateral femoral condyle has been described as a landmark beyond which the femoral component should not be sized or implanted. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Arthroplasty Techniques -- Technique or Treatment -- Procedural Steps
retrieved_docs.content: Following that, the intercondylar notch cut is performed perpendicular to the transepicondylar axis. Attention is then directed back to the proximal tibia to complete preparation, sizing, and rotational alignment. Care must be taken to avoid internal rotation and component overhang, as these can result in suboptimal TKA outcomes. Subsequently, the femoral and tibial trial implants are impacted, and a provisional spacer trial is inserted. The knee is reduced and evaluated for stability from 0° of extension through mid-flexion stability.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Arthroplasty Techniques -- Technique or Treatment -- Procedural Steps
retrieved_docs.content: A spacer block can facilitate this assessment. A surgeon must verify that a rectangular flexion gap will be achieved after the bone resections. Once satisfactory checks and balancing steps are confirmed, the anterior, posterior, and anterior and posterior chamfer cuts are executed. Care must be taken to protect the collateral soft tissue structures, such as the lateral collateral ligament and medial collateral ligament, using retractors.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Arthroplasty Techniques -- Technique or Treatment -- Hot Topics in TKA Techniques
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior versus posterior referencing: The correct positioning and sizing of the femoral component ensure proper kinematic function in TKA. Anterior and posterior referencing are the 2 main strategies for setting the center of rotation and sagittal plane balancing in TKA, based on the reference used to determine the size and geometry of distal femur resection. [31] In anterior referencing, the anterior femoral cortex is the reference, whereas the resection of the posterior femoral condyle varies, posing challenges in flexion space balancing. Anterior referencing helps reduce the risk of AFC notching and patellofemoral joint overstuffing. However, it increases the risks of flexion gap instability with excessive posterior condylar resection or femoral offset reduction. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Replacement Techniques -- Technique or Treatment -- 1) Measured Resection
retrieved_docs.content: There is an increased incidence of femoral condylar liftoff in this technique compared to gap balancing.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Replacement Techniques -- Technique or Treatment -- 1) Measured Resection
retrieved_docs.content: The pitfall of the technique is that it is difficult to perform soft-tissue releases after performing bony cuts. Ligament releases after bony cuts can affect the flexion/extension gaps and lead to poor balancing. Changing ligament tension in extension will affect the flexion gap and vice versa.

Procedures that modify femoral length or geometry are classified as having a procedural site rather than a direct site and comprise osteoplasty of the femur for lengthening, osteoplasty of the femur for shortening, and combined lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer, which are employed to correct limb‑length discrepancy and angular deformities. Indirect femoral interventions include replacement of a femoral prosthesis using methyl methacrylate, revision of total knee arthroplasty (all components), and prophylactic treatment of the femur; these are performed as part of broader reconstructive or prosthetic strategies rather than primary bone fixation. Growth‑modulating procedures linked to the femur through a “has relationship” connection are epiphyseal arrest (any method, e.g., epiphysiodesis) of the distal femur and hemiepiphyseal arrest of the distal femur, proximal tibia or fibula, which are used to halt

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: of femur,” and “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation.” Osteoplastic procedures modify femoral length or contour and include “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and the combined “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer.” Preventive and reconstructive measures are captured by “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion of femur distal to head and neck,” “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur” (soft‑tissue repair). Joint‑related reconstruction is addressed by “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure),” while “Trochanterplasty” reshapes or repositions the greater trochanter. The Arabic‑language child procedure “إعاضة رأس الفخذ” (femoral‑head reconstruction) further expands the femoral‑head therapeutic options. Clinical mapping links a closed intracapsular femoral

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 122
retrieved_docs.content: **Trochanterplasty** and **femoral‑head reconstruction (إعاضة رأس الفخذ)** are also performed directly on the femur. Growth‑plate modulation techniques such as **arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis) – combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula**, **arrest, epiphyseal, distal femur**, and **arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)** target the distal femur, proximal tibia, or fibula to correct angular deformities (genu varus/valgus). Indirect‑site interventions linked to the femur include **replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate**, **revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components**, and **suture of periosteum of femur**. Preventive care is represented by **prophylactic treatment of femur**. The procedure **internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction** (Arabic: تثبيت داخلي للكسر), a subclass of internal fixation of clavicle without fracture reduction, is applied to both

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 754
retrieved_docs.content: A wide range of orthopedic interventions are linked to the femur, reflecting the bone’s central role in lower‑extremity surgery. The procedure described as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula” is associated with the femur, indicating that epiphyseal arrest techniques may involve the distal femur in conjunction with the proximal tibia and fibula. Direct surgical actions on the femur include “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” and “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” each of which specifies the femur as the direct procedure site. Procedures that are performed on or modify the femur more generally are identified as having the femur as the procedure site, such as “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Interventions that involve the femur in a less direct manner are classified with an indirect relationship, exemplified by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Suture of periosteum of femur,” both of which denote the femur as an indirect procedure site. Together, these relationships delineate how various surgical techniques—ranging from fracture fixation and bone shortening to prosthetic revision and soft‑tissue repair—are organized around the femur, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding femoral‑focused orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 154

Prosthetic and joint‑related interventions involve “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate,” “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure),” and “Trochanterplasty,” which address femoral implant revision, comprehensive knee arthroplasty component exchange, and greater trochanter reshaping to improve hip biomechanics. Collectively, these procedures illustrate the extensive, femur‑centered surgical repertoire for managing growth disturbances, fractures, malunions, deformities, and prosthetic complications, emphasizing the need for individualized operative planning based on anatomical site, underlying pathology, and desired functional outcome.

Reconstructive and prophylactic measures include revision of total knee arthroplasty (all components), replacement of a femoral prosthesis with methyl methacrylate, prophylactic treatment of the femur, and trochanterplasty. These procedures are linked to broader parent categories of treatment for fracture or dislocation of the hip and femur and for fracture or dislocation of the lower extremity (other than hip or femur), underscoring their role in managing acute trauma as well as chronic deformity. Internal fixation without fracture reduction—applied to the hip, femur, other lower‑extremity sites, and the clavicle—shares technique across skeletal regions and is grouped with hip‑head replacement and multiple fractures within the osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام) clinical category, reflecting

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 979
retrieved_docs.content: The category THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS, which is classified under the broader treatment of fracture or dislocation of the lower extremity (other than hip or femur), comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions. These interventions include arrest, epiphyseal, any method (e.g., epiphysiodesis) of the distal femur; arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (e.g., for genu varus or valgus); femoral shortening with blade plate; internal fixation of femur; open reduction of fracture of femur; osteoplasty of femur, lengthening; prophylactic treatment of femur; replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate; and revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure). Together, these procedures represent the surgical repertoire for addressing complex thigh and leg injuries, deformities, and prosthetic revisions, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate reparative and reconstructive strategies for optimal patient care.

Preventive and reparative measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion of femur distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” “Trochanterplasty” reshapes the greater trochanter to improve hip biomechanics. Additionally, “Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is identified as a versatile internal fixation technique applicable to fractures or dislocations of the hip

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1045
retrieved_docs.content: Necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb represents a necrotic morphological change (النَخَر) and is commonly associated with an amputation of the lower extremity. The category of amputation of the lower extremity includes various procedural levels, one of which is the above‑knee amputation (بتر فوق الركبة). Recognizing that necrosis of the stump can occur after lower‑extremity amputation underscores the need for careful postoperative monitoring and early intervention to prevent further tissue loss and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2906
retrieved_docs.content: Amputation of lower extremity is a surgical procedure that encompasses distinct child operations, specifically بتر تحت الركبة (below‑knee amputation) and بتر فوق الركبة (above‑knee amputation). These two levels of amputation represent different anatomical sites of limb removal, each influencing postoperative function, prosthetic fitting, and rehabilitation strategies for the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2207
retrieved_docs.content: The patient’s surgical history that is relevant to physical‑therapy treatment includes a below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة) and a peripheral vascular bypass. Both procedures are recorded as types of surgeries performed at specific time points in the patient’s record and are documented within the “surgical history related to physical‑therapy treatment: type, time point, patient (nominal type)” description. This information is important for planning and delivering appropriate physical‑therapy interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 989
retrieved_docs.content: THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS is classified as a subcategory of treatment; fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur). Within this category, a range of specific operative interventions are enumerated, including Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (e.g., genu varus or valgus); internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction; open reduction of fracture of femur; open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation; osteoplasty of femur; osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer; repair of malunion of femur distal to head and neck; revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure); and trochanterplasty. These procedures address various deformities, fractures, malunions, and prosthetic revisions of the thigh and leg, providing surgical options for correction, stabilization, and reconstruction. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship aids clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical interventions within the broader context of lower‑extremity trauma and orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1992
retrieved_docs.content: Amputation of lower extremity is a broad surgical category that includes specific procedures such as بتر أصبع القدم (toe amputation) and بتر تحت الركبة (below‑knee amputation). A notable complication associated with these procedures is necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb, which is directly linked to the amputation of lower extremity. This necrotic condition is characterized by the morphological finding النَخَر (necrosis). The relationship between lower‑extremity amputation and subsequent stump necrosis underscores the importance of monitoring for tissue viability and managing potential complications in patients undergoing toe or below‑knee amputations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2187
retrieved_docs.content: Necrosis of amputation stump of right lower limb is described as an associated morphology of النَخَر and is closely linked with the broader concept of amputation of lower extremity. The category of amputation of lower extremity encompasses two specific procedural subtypes: بتر تحت الركبة (below‑knee amputation) and بتر فوق الركبة (above‑knee amputation). Understanding that necrosis of the stump can occur in the context of these lower‑extremity amputations highlights the importance of monitoring stump health and recognizing morphological changes such as those related to النَخَر in postoperative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1878
retrieved_docs.content: The head of the first metatarsal bone is an anatomical structure that is characterized by laterality, meaning it can be identified as belonging to either the left or right foot. This region also serves as the direct procedural site for bunionectomy or repair of toe deformities, indicating its central role in surgical interventions aimed at correcting hallux valgus and other toe malalignments. Recognizing both the side‑specific nature of the head of the first metatarsal bone and its function as the operative target is essential for accurate pre‑operative planning and effective patient care in foot and ankle surgery.
